Assignment 5: Mixed models

Boglarka Ludmany

Introduction

This assignment is related to the previous lab assignment concerning perioperative pain and its psychological and hormonal predictors. Just like previously, you will set up linear model to predict postoperative pain after wisdom tooth surgery, but this time you will have to also take into account the fact that there is clustering in the data.

Your research paper on the effect of the psychological and hormonal predictors of postoperative pain was so successful, that you managed to secure research funding for a multi-site replication study. Here your collaborators collect data in the same way you did in the original study at 20 different hospital sites. The goal of the study is to increase the generalizability of your findings. You would like to assess the model coefficients and the overall predictive efficiency of the predictors in your model.

As a reminder, here is the protocol for data collection: “You have collected data from adults who were scheduled to undergo surgical extraction of the third mandibular molar (wisdom tooth surgery). Patients filled out a form in the waiting room before their surgery. The form contained questions about their sex, age, and weight, and psychological questionnaires assessing anxiety, pain catastrophizing, and mindfulness (see descriptions below). You also got blood samples and saliva samples from participants in the waiting room 5 minutes before their operations to determine the serum (a component of the blood) and salivary cortisol levels of participants. Participants were contacted 5 hours after the surgery to see how much pain they were experiencing. The level of pain at that moment was recorded using a numerical rating scale using a scale of 0 to 10, where 0 means “no pain” and 10 means “worst pain I can imagine”.

The State Trait Anxiety Inventory: T measures trait anxiety on a scale of 20 to 80, higher scores mean higher anxiety. Anxiety has been found in many studies to positively correlate with the level of pain experienced. This is variable STAI_trait in the dataset.

The Pain Catastrophizing Scale measures the extent of pain catastrophizing, which is characterized by a tendency to magnify the threat value of a pain stimulus and to feel helpless in the presence of pain, as well as by a relative inability to prevent or inhibit pain-related thoughts in anticipation of, during, or following a painful event. The total score on this scale ranges from 0 to 52, higher scores mean higher catastrophizing. Pain catastrophizing is one of the well-established predictors of clinical pain. This is variable pain_cat in the dataset.

The Mindful Attention Awareness Scale (MAAS) measures dispositional mindfulness, which may be described as a tendency to turn attention to present-moment experiences in an open, non-judgemental way. The MAAS total score ranges from 1 to 6 (an average of the item scores), with higher scores representing higher dispositional mindfulness. Trait mindfulness has been theorized to serve as a protective factor against pain, as the individual would be more objective about their pain experience and tend to associate less discomfort, despair, and hopelessness to the pain-related sensations. This is variable mindfulness in the dataset.

Cortisol is a stress hormone associated with acute and chronic stress. Cortisol levels are thought to be positively associated with pain experience. Cortisol can be measured from both blood and the saliva, although, serum cortisol is often regarded in medical research as more reliably related to stress (serum is a component of the blood plasma). These are variables cortisol_serum, and cortisol_saliva in the dataset.”

Datasets

You will need two datasets for this assignment, datafile A and B. You can load them from the ‘data/’ folder.

Task

First, fit a linear mixed model to estimate postoperative pain on datafile A. You should use the same fixed effect predictors as you used in your final model in the 3 assignment. (If you did not do that assignment, use the following predictors: age, sex, STAI, pain catastrophizing, mindfulness, and serum cortisol.) Importantly, the model has to account for the clustering of the data in different hospital sites. We have no reason to assume that the effects of the different predictors would be different in the different hospitals, so fit a random intercept model including the random intercept of hospital-ID. Once the model is built, note the model coefficients and the confidence intervals of the coefficients for all fixed effect predictors, and compare them to the ones obtained in the 3 assignment.

Also, compute the variance explained by the fixed effect predictors using marginal R2, and the variance explained by the fixed and random effect terms combined using conditional R2. Now use the model coefficients obtained on data file A to predict pain in datafile B.

IMPORTANT: Do not fit the regression models on data file B (don’t re-train your models), just use the regression equation you derived based on datafile A. These regression equations should be applied on the new data (datafile B), to predict pain.

Now compute the variance explained by the model on datafile B. You can do this by using the formula: 1 - (RSS / TSS) = R^2. Compare this R2 to the marginal and conditional R2 values computed for the model on datafile A.

What to report

Report the model coefficients and the confidence intervals of the coefficients for each fixed effect predictor obtained on data file A in a table.

Report the variance components for the fixed effects, the random intercept, and the residuals (from the model on data file A). Also report the marginal R2 and the conditional R2 squared obtained from the model on data file A, and the observed R2 of this model for data file B.

What to discuss

Compare the model coefficients and the confidence intervals observed in this assignment and the assignment for Lab 2 and discuss what you think the differences or similarities mean.

Solution

Read the data

Read the datasets used in this assignment. Pay attention to the extensions of the datafiles.

Load libraries

library(broom.mixed)
## Warning: package 'broom.mixed' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(performance)
## Warning: package 'performance' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(corrplot)
## corrplot 0.95 loaded
library(haven)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(magrittr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'magrittr'
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     set_names
## The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     extract
library(ggplot2)
library(lmerTest)
## Loading required package: lme4
## Loading required package: Matrix
## 
## Attaching package: 'Matrix'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     expand, pack, unpack
## 
## Attaching package: 'lmerTest'
## The following object is masked from 'package:lme4':
## 
##     lmer
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     step
library(lme4)
library(apyramid)

===================================================================================================

DATASET A - EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

# Dataset A: 
a_raw_data <- haven::read_spss("../data/assignment_4_dataset_A.sav")

Exploratory data analysis

Run an exploratory data analysis (EDA) to investigate the dataset.

1. Data structure

The first step was to investigate the structure of the dataset: this included the types and ranges of variables, and the potential presence of missing values.

Data types

str(a_raw_data) 
## tibble [200 × 13] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
##  $ ID              : chr [1:200] "ID_1" "ID_2" "ID_3" "ID_4" ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "A6"
##   ..- attr(*, "display_width")= int 6
##  $ pain            : num [1:200] 6 7 5 5 7 4 3 6 5 3 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F2.0"
##  $ sex             : chr [1:200] "male" "female" "male" "female" ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "A6"
##   ..- attr(*, "display_width")= int 6
##  $ age             : num [1:200] 31 42 35 37 37 39 47 33 46 33 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F2.0"
##  $ STAI_trait      : num [1:200] 45 50 36 35 46 38 40 39 39 32 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F2.0"
##  $ pain_cat        : num [1:200] 34 31 30 26 38 28 27 32 28 22 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F2.0"
##  $ cortisol_serum  : num [1:200] 4.59 7.11 5.2 3.62 6.17 3.69 3.97 4.96 5.49 4.77 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F4.2"
##  $ cortisol_saliva : num [1:200] 5.31 7.11 5.24 3.95 6.54 3.3 3.43 4.48 4.7 4.92 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F4.2"
##  $ mindfulness     : num [1:200] 1 2.77 2.17 2.06 1 3.83 4.56 4.06 3.84 5.45 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F4.2"
##  $ weight          : num [1:200] 72.7 71 56.7 75.9 61.9 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F5.2"
##  $ IQ              : num [1:200] 97 98 79 81 94 76 104 110 95 94 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F3.0"
##  $ household_income: num [1:200] 42438 79344 86756 55015 92577 ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "F6.0"
##  $ hospital        : chr [1:200] "hospital_1" "hospital_1" "hospital_1" "hospital_1" ...
##   ..- attr(*, "format.spss")= chr "A11"
##   ..- attr(*, "display_width")= int 11

The variables are mostly numerical (pain, age, STAI_trait, pain_cat, cortisol_serum, cortisol_saliva, mindfulness, weight, IQ, household_income), while strings can also be found in the dataset (ID, sex, hospital).

Missing values

colSums(is.na(a_raw_data))
##               ID             pain              sex              age 
##                0                0                0                0 
##       STAI_trait         pain_cat   cortisol_serum  cortisol_saliva 
##                0                0                0                0 
##      mindfulness           weight               IQ household_income 
##                0                0                0                0 
##         hospital 
##                0

No missing data was found within the variables.

Value ranges

summary(a_raw_data)
##       ID                 pain            sex                 age       
##  Length:200         Min.   : 0.000   Length:200         Min.   :28.00  
##  Class :character   1st Qu.: 4.000   Class :character   1st Qu.:37.00  
##  Mode  :character   Median : 5.000   Mode  :character   Median :40.00  
##                     Mean   : 4.845                      Mean   :39.99  
##                     3rd Qu.: 6.000                      3rd Qu.:43.00  
##                     Max.   :10.000                      Max.   :51.00  
##    STAI_trait       pain_cat     cortisol_serum  cortisol_saliva
##  Min.   :24.00   Min.   :17.00   Min.   :1.640   Min.   :2.090  
##  1st Qu.:36.00   1st Qu.:28.00   1st Qu.:4.290   1st Qu.:4.175  
##  Median :40.00   Median :30.00   Median :4.970   Median :4.965  
##  Mean   :39.66   Mean   :29.98   Mean   :4.914   Mean   :4.901  
##  3rd Qu.:43.00   3rd Qu.:33.00   3rd Qu.:5.540   3rd Qu.:5.580  
##  Max.   :52.00   Max.   :41.00   Max.   :7.630   Max.   :7.910  
##   mindfulness        weight            IQ         household_income
##  Min.   :1.000   Min.   :38.80   Min.   : 59.00   Min.   : -2672  
##  1st Qu.:2.265   1st Qu.:62.42   1st Qu.: 90.00   1st Qu.: 51175  
##  Median :2.910   Median :69.55   Median :100.00   Median : 66268  
##  Mean   :2.998   Mean   :69.57   Mean   : 99.86   Mean   : 68755  
##  3rd Qu.:3.830   3rd Qu.:75.58   3rd Qu.:110.00   3rd Qu.: 87047  
##  Max.   :5.580   Max.   :93.08   Max.   :148.00   Max.   :151242  
##    hospital        
##  Length:200        
##  Class :character  
##  Mode  :character  
##                    
##                    
##

All the variables were in synch with the task description, the ranges were identical to the ones mentioned above.

2. Distributions

2.1. Distribution of postoperative pain scores (pain)

ggplot(a_raw_data, aes(x = pain)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of prosperative pain scores"
  )
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

The scores appeared to be approximately symmetrically dirstibuted. The distribution shows no strong skewness, and can be considered approximately normal.

2.2. Psychological predictors

Next, the distributions of the following psychological variables were examined: * STAI_trait (trait anxiety), * pain_cat (pain catastrophizing), * mindfulness.

Trait anxiety - STAI_trait
range(a_raw_data$STAI_trait)
## [1] 24 52
# Range is between 24 and 52. 
# The variable measures anxiety on a scale of 20-80.

ggplot(a_raw_data, aes(x = STAI_trait)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(20, 60)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of trait anxiety")
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_bar()`).

# The theoretical maximum limit of the scale are not displayed on the axis.

The distribution appears to have a slight right skew - indicating more observations at higher levels of anxiety. No extreme outliers were observed. The maximum observed value was 52, considerably below the theoretical upper bound of the scale (80). This suggests that very high levels of trait anxiety were not present in this sample.

Pain catastrophizing - pain_cat
range(a_raw_data$pain_cat)
## [1] 17 41
# Range is between 17 and 42. Theoretical minimum on the scale is 0, the theoretical maximum is 52. 

ggplot(a_raw_data, aes(x = pain_cat)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 52)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of pain catastrophizing")
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_bar()`).

# The x axis limits reflect the theoretical range of the pain catastrophizing scale.

The distribution show that majority of the observations are concentrated between approximately 25 and 35, indicating an overally moderate level of catastrophizing.

Mindfulness
range(a_raw_data$mindfulness)
## [1] 1.00 5.58
# Range is between 1.00 and 5.58, which is in connection with the theoretical range of 1-6.

ggplot(a_raw_data, aes(x = mindfulness)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), 
                 binwidth = 0.1, 
                 fill = "#2f9147") +
  geom_density() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of dispositional mindfulness")

The distribution of dispositional mindfulness was visualized using a histogram. In order to show the shape of the distribution more smoothly, a density line was added to the histogram. Most observations are concentrated between approximately 2.5 and 4, indicating mild levels of dispositional mindfulness in the sample. Considerably high values are relatively infrequent, no clear extreme outliers can be detected.

2.3. Hormonal variables - cortisol

The distributions of cortisol_serum and cortisol_saliva were also visualized with a faceted histograms and density curves.

cortisol_data <- a_raw_data %>% 
  select(cortisol_serum, cortisol_saliva) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), 
               names_to = "type", 
               values_to = "cortisol")

Here, the data was formatted in order to create the plot.

ggplot(cortisol_data, aes(x = cortisol)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), 
                 binwidth = 0.15, 
                 fill = "#328da8") +
  geom_density() +
  facet_wrap(~ type) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of cortisol levels")

The distributions show similar shape. However, the serum cortisol values seem to be centered at a slightly higher level, compared to salivary cortisol.

2.4. Demographic variables

Finally, the distributions of the following demographic variables were visualized: * age, * sex, * and weight.

age_sex_data <- a_raw_data %>% 
  select(age, sex) %>% 
  mutate(age = as.factor(age)) %>% 
  mutate(sex = recode(sex, "Male" = "male"))

age_pyramid(age_sex_data, age_group = age, split_by = sex) +
      labs(x = "Age group", 
          y = "Number of people", 
          title = "Distribution of age within the genders")

The age distribution of participants was visualized separately for female and male participants. Ages ranged approximately from 28 to 51 years. The distributions for males and females appear to be similar, no clear systematic difference can be observed.

Visualization of weight within males and females:

sex_weight_data <- a_raw_data %>% 
  select(weight, sex) %>% 
  mutate(sex = recode(sex, "Male" = "male"))

ggplot(sex_weight_data, aes(x = sex, y = weight)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, 
              alpha = 0.4)

3. Bivariate relationships - pain and predictors

3.1. Pain ~ STAI_trait

stai_pain_data <- a_raw_data %>%
  group_by(pain) %>%
  summarise(mean_STAI = mean(STAI_trait),
            se = sd(STAI_trait)/sqrt(n())) 
  ggplot(stai_pain_data, aes(x = pain, y = mean_STAI)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_STAI - se,
                    ymax = mean_STAI + se))

For each pain level (1-10), the average STAI (trait anxiety) was plotted, with the error bars. A positive trend is visible, which suggests that lower pain is associated with lower anxiety, while as pain increases, anxiety also increases.

3.2. Pain ~ pain_cat

pain_cat_data <- a_raw_data %>%
  group_by(pain) %>%
  summarise(mean_pain_cat = mean(pain_cat),
            se = sd(pain_cat)/sqrt(n()))

 ggplot(pain_cat_data, aes(x = pain, y = mean_pain_cat)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_pain_cat - se,
                    ymax = mean_pain_cat + se))

The plot visualizes mean pain catastrophizing for each pain level, with error bars. As pain increases, mean pain catastrophizing also increases, which shows a clean positive relationship. Some variablity is present at certain pain levels - this might reflect differences in sample size across categories.

3.3. Pain ~ mindfulness

mindfulness_data <- a_raw_data %>%
  group_by(pain) %>%
  summarise(mean_mindfulness = mean(mindfulness),
            se = sd(mindfulness)/sqrt(n()))

 ggplot(mindfulness_data, aes(x = pain, y = mean_mindfulness)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_mindfulness - se,
                    ymax = mean_mindfulness + se))

3.4. Pain ~ cortisol_serum & Pain ~ cortisol_saliva

cortisol_long_data <- a_raw_data %>%
  select(pain, cortisol_serum, cortisol_saliva) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -pain,
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value"
  )

cortisol_summary_data <- cortisol_long_data %>%
  group_by(variable, pain) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(cortisol_summary_data, aes(x = pain, y = mean_value)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_value - se,
                    ymax = mean_value + se),
                width = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Cortisol level across pain levels",
    x = "Pain",
    y = "Mean value"
  )

In both panels, a clear positive trend is observed: higher pain levels are associated with higher average cortisol levels.

4. Relationships among predictors

Predictors are the following: * STAI_trait * pain_cat * mindfulness * cortisol_serum * cortisol_saliva * age * weight

In order to investigate the relationships between the predictors, a correlation matrix was created.

cor_data <- a_raw_data %>% 
  select(STAI_trait, pain_cat, mindfulness,
         cortisol_serum, cortisol_saliva,
         age, weight)

cor_matrix <- cor(cor_data, use = "complete.obs")

cor_matrix
##                  STAI_trait   pain_cat mindfulness cortisol_serum
## STAI_trait       1.00000000  0.5046613 -0.34335918     0.48986914
## pain_cat         0.50466134  1.0000000 -0.56660008     0.38200518
## mindfulness     -0.34335918 -0.5666001  1.00000000    -0.22025899
## cortisol_serum   0.48986914  0.3820052 -0.22025899     1.00000000
## cortisol_saliva  0.52098011  0.3146115 -0.25265263     0.88190933
## age              0.08442875 -0.1714817  0.04404146    -0.23178450
## weight          -0.07786800 -0.1251127  0.13558433    -0.03860455
##                 cortisol_saliva         age      weight
## STAI_trait           0.52098011  0.08442875 -0.07786800
## pain_cat             0.31461153 -0.17148172 -0.12511268
## mindfulness         -0.25265263  0.04404146  0.13558433
## cortisol_serum       0.88190933 -0.23178450 -0.03860455
## cortisol_saliva      1.00000000 -0.33268616 -0.01642476
## age                 -0.33268616  1.00000000 -0.11149708
## weight              -0.01642476 -0.11149708  1.00000000

Visualization:

corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper")

Pbsychological predictors showed moderate levels of correlation: trait anxiety and pain catastrophizing correlated positively (r = 0.5047), mindfulness and pain catastrophizing correlated negatively (-0.5666), which indicates that lower levels of mindfulness is linked to higher levels of pain catastrophizing. Conversely, associations between psychological biological preditors were generally weak.

5. Differences between hospital sites

In order to assess the potential differences between the hospital sites with regards to the outcome variable (pain), the pain levels were examined across the hospital sites.

ggplot(a_raw_data, aes(x = hospital, y = pain)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Based on the boxplot, a small level of variation between hospital sites can be observed; however, the differences appear to be relatively moderate.

Before modeling, search answers for: - should I transform (log)? - should i stardardize predictors?

=============================================================

Correct coding errors

If you find values in the dataset during the EDA, that are not correct based on the provided descriptions of the variables of the dataset please correct them here.

a_raw_data <- a_raw_data %>%
  mutate(sex = recode(sex, "Male" = "male"),
         hospital = as.factor(hospital))

=============================================================

Linear Mixed Model on Dataset A

A linear mixed model was fitted with postoperatice pain as the outcome, and age, sex, trait anxiety (STAI), pain catastrophizing were added as fixed effect predictors. A random intercept model was fitted, including hospital-ID, in order to account for the slight fluctuation of pain levels across hospital sites.

model_A <- lmer(
  pain ~ age + sex + STAI_trait + pain_cat + mindfulness + cortisol_serum +
    (1 | hospital),
  data = a_raw_data
)
  1. Fixed effects results Extracting the coefficients and the CIs:
summary(model_A)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: 
## pain ~ age + sex + STAI_trait + pain_cat + mindfulness + cortisol_serum +  
##     (1 | hospital)
##    Data: a_raw_data
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 682.6
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -3.10080 -0.67358  0.05678  0.66353  2.78802 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  hospital (Intercept) 0.3131   0.5596  
##  Residual             1.4866   1.2193  
## Number of obs: 200, groups:  hospital, 10
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                  Estimate Std. Error         df t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)      3.502306   1.380454 192.019684   2.537  0.01197 *  
## age             -0.054400   0.019818 189.297615  -2.745  0.00664 ** 
## sexmale          0.297545   0.190460 187.406434   1.562  0.11992    
## STAI_trait       0.001437   0.023851 185.932388   0.060  0.95202    
## pain_cat         0.036885   0.028633 187.343455   1.288  0.19926    
## mindfulness     -0.262441   0.100647 187.056391  -2.608  0.00986 ** 
## cortisol_serum   0.610010   0.114260 186.705239   5.339  2.7e-07 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr) age    sexmal STAI_t pan_ct mndfln
## age         -0.626                                   
## sexmale      0.144 -0.206                            
## STAI_trait  -0.104 -0.321  0.214                     
## pain_cat    -0.552  0.269 -0.358 -0.400              
## mindfulness -0.569  0.056 -0.076  0.032  0.483       
## cortisl_srm -0.224  0.261  0.001 -0.416 -0.078  0.003
confint(model_A, method = "Wald")
##                      2.5 %      97.5 %
## .sig01                  NA          NA
## .sigma                  NA          NA
## (Intercept)     0.79666571  6.20794562
## age            -0.09324285 -0.01555684
## sexmale        -0.07574983  0.67083962
## STAI_trait     -0.04531105  0.04818505
## pain_cat       -0.01923450  0.09300448
## mindfulness    -0.45970466 -0.06517714
## cortisol_serum  0.38606414  0.83395592

Among the predictors, serum cortisol showed a strong positive relationship with pain (β = 0.61, 95% CI [0.39, 0.83], p < .001), which indicates that higher cortisol levels are associated with higher pain levels. Mindfulness negatively correlated with postoperative pain (β = -0.26, 95% CI [-0.46, -0.07], p = .001), suggesting that lower pain can be linked to higher mindfulness. Age showed a considerably small, but significant correlation with postoperative pain (β = -0.05, 95% CI [-0.09, -0.02], p = .007).

Conversely, sex (β = 0.30, 95% CI [-0.08, 0.67], p = .120), trait anxiety (β = 0.001, 95% CI [-0.045, 0.048], p = .952), and pain catastrophizing (β = 0.04, 95% CI [-0.02, 0.09], p = .199) are not significantly associated with pain.

The variance of the random intercept of hospital (σ² = 0.31) was smaller than the residual variance, indicating that majority of the variability in pain occured at the individual-level.

tidy_model <- broom.mixed::tidy(model_A, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE)

library(knitr)
## Warning: package 'knitr' was built under R version 4.5.2
tidy_model %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = round(p.value, 3)
  ) %>%
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) %>%
  rename(
    Predictor = term,
    Beta = estimate,
    `CI Lower` = conf.low,
    `CI Upper` = conf.high,
    `p-value` = p.value
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Fixed effects of the linear mixed-effects model")
Fixed effects of the linear mixed-effects model
Predictor Beta CI Lower CI Upper p-value
(Intercept) 3.50 0.78 6.23 0.012
age -0.05 -0.09 -0.02 0.007
sexmale 0.30 -0.08 0.67 0.120
STAI_trait 0.00 -0.05 0.05 0.952
pain_cat 0.04 -0.02 0.09 0.199
mindfulness -0.26 -0.46 -0.06 0.010
cortisol_serum 0.61 0.38 0.84 0.000

The fixed effect estimates of the model are presented in the table, including the Beta values, the 95% confidence intervals, and the p values.

Variance components:

VarCorr(model_A)
##  Groups   Name        Std.Dev.
##  hospital (Intercept) 0.55956 
##  Residual             1.21928

The standard deviation of the random intercept was 0.56, and the residual standard deviation was 1.22.

Marginal and Conditional R squared

r2_values <- performance::r2(model_A)
r2_values
## # R2 for Mixed Models
## 
##   Conditional R2: 0.436
##      Marginal R2: 0.317

The marginal R squared indicated that 31.7% of the variance was explained by the fixed effect predictors. The conditional R was higher (43.6%), suggesting that the random intercept structure improved the model (this model explains the variance in a stronger manner).

=============================================================

DATASET B

Importing the data:

b_raw_data <- haven::read_spss("../data/assignment_4_dataset_B.sav")

Generating predictions:

predict_b <- predict(model_A, newdata = b_raw_data, re.form = NA)

In order to evaluate the generalizability of the model used on dataset A, predictions were generated for dataset B, using the fixed effects estimated from dataset A.

Computing R squared:

RSS <- sum((b_raw_data$pain - predict_b)^2)
TSS <- sum((b_raw_data$pain - mean(b_raw_data$pain))^2)
R2_B <- 1 - (RSS / TSS)
R2_B
## [1] 0.3497686

Based on the calculations (using the formula R2 = 1 − (TSS/RSS)), the R squared value is 0.35, indicating that this model explained approximately 35% of the variance in pain in dataset B. The R squared value is lower than in dataset A (the conditional R squared was 0.436), suggesting that the model’s predictive performance decreased when applied to new data.
